title: How to Show an Apartment: Lifestyle, Not Square Footage
dek: The most common showing mistake in real estate, at every experience level: narrating the floor plan. "Here's the kitchen. Stainless appliances. Second bedroom down the hall." The client has eyes. They can see the kitchen.
heroAlt: How to Show an Apartment: Lifestyle, Not Square Footage

blocks.html: Nobody rents square footage. They rent a life. The unit is the container; the product is Tuesday evening. Your job on a tour is to show them living there, not to inventory the finishes.

blocks.text: Sell the Tuesday evening, not the floor plan.
Every feature translates into a moment. Practice the conversion:

blocks.html: West-facing windows becomes "you get the sunset over the skyline with your dinner." The rooftop deck becomes "this is where you're bringing people your first weekend here. Everyone does. It's the flex." In-unit laundry becomes "Sunday errands disappear." Walkable location becomes "your commute is a podcast and a coffee."

The same rooftop is three different products. To a young professional it's status and social life. To a relocating executive it's a place to decompress in an unfamiliar city. To a lifestyle upgrader it's outdoor space with zero maintenance. Know who's in front of you (tomorrow's article covers the three renter types) and translate accordingly.

One more craft note: stop talking at the peak moments. Open the door to the view, then be quiet. The best showings alternate between vivid translation and deliberate silence, because the moment a client starts mentally arranging their furniture, your job is to not interrupt.
The full tour structure, room by room, is in The Luxury Leasing Playbook, twenty dollars. Better agents win.

blocks.lead: The field manual, day by day.
